beats.prompt: Match each result to its value — E[pattern] means the expected (average) wait for that pattern. Tap a result on the left, then its match on the right.

feedback.correct: All matched — those are the headline numbers of the course.
hints: Tap a result, then its value.
HH waited longer than HT; THH is longer still.

beats.prompt: Read why mixed practice works, then continue.

interaction.title: Mixed beats blocked
interaction.body: Practicing different kinds of problems together feels harder than drilling one kind — and that's exactly why it works better. You're learning to tell them apart, the way a real problem arrives without a label.

beats.prompt: Match each pattern to its average wait. Which one waits the longest?

feedback.correct: Right — HTH waits 10, the longest here. Structure, not length, sets the wait.

beats.prompt: Two friends watch ONE coin: one waits for HH, the other for HT. Press Run to watch one race, then run the batch — who appears first more often?

hero.structuralReadout: On one shared stream, HH and HT each win about 50 of every 100 races — a dead tie.

feedback.correct: A tie — "who's first" on a shared stream isn't "who's faster alone."
hints: This is a race, not a solo wait.

beats.prompt: Match each kind of recurrence — the rule linking a state to its neighbors — to its per-flip cost.

feedback.correct: Right — durations pay 1 per flip; a probability has no per-flip cost.
hints: One counts flips, one splits chances.

beats.prompt: On the HH machine: you've matched a lone H (state E1). Tap where each next flip sends you.

feedback.correct: Reset — and that extra restart is exactly why HH (6) beats HT (4).
hints: A T breaks the HH streak.

beats.prompt: A fair coin is flipped until \(\text{HTT}\) (heads, then two tails) first appears. What is the average number of flips? Type a whole number.

interaction.scenario: Build the state machine and read off where each flip sends you. The twist: after you've matched \(\text{HT}\), a head doesn't send you all the way back — you still hold one head, so you only fall back to "one H matched."

feedback.correct: \(E[\text{HTT}] = 8\). Name the states by progress: \(E_0\) (nothing), \(E_1\) (one H), \(E_2\) (matched HT), \(E_3 = 0\) (done). First-step analysis writes one equation per state from where the next flip lands: \[ E_0 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_0, \] \[ E_1 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_2, \] \[ E_2 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_3. \] The key edge is from \(E_2\): a tail finishes (\(E_3\)), but a head does NOT reset to \(E_0\) — it lands back on \(E_1\), because that head is itself a fresh start of \(\text{H}\)\(\dots\). Back-substitute \(E_2 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_1\) into \(E_1\): \(E_1 = \tfrac{3}{2} + \tfrac{3}{4}E_1\), so \(\tfrac{1}{4}E_1 = \tfrac{3}{2}\) and \(E_1 = 6\); then \(E_2 = 4\) and \(E_0 = 1 + 3 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_0\), giving \(E[\text{HTT}] = 8\). Same first-step method as \(\text{TT}\) — only the partial-reset edge changes the answer.
hints: First-step analysis: define a state for each amount of progress (\(E_0\) none, \(E_1\) one H, \(E_2\) matched HT) and write one equation per state for the average flips remaining.
Watch the \(E_2\) edge carefully. From "matched HT," a tail finishes the pattern, but a head does not send you back to zero — you still hold a single head, so you drop to \(E_1\), not \(E_0\).

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: each part uses a different tool - a pattern wait or a walk between two walls. Decide which, then compute.

interaction.scenario: Part A: a fair coin is flipped until \(\text{TT}\) (two tails in a row) first appears. Part B (separate game): a chip starts at \(\$3\) with walls at \(\$0\) and \(\$8\); each flip moves it \(+\$1\) on heads, \(-\$1\) on tails until it hits a wall.

fields.label: Part B - chance the chip reaches \(\$8\) before \(\$0\)

feedback.correct: Part A = \(6\), Part B = \(\tfrac{3}{8}\). Part A is a pattern wait: \(\text{TT}\) has no self-overlap other than its full length, so a single H resets all progress just like \(\text{HH}\). The wait equations are \[ E_0 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_1, \] \[ E_1 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_0. \] Substituting \(E_1\) into \(E_0\): \(E_0 = \tfrac{3}{2} + \tfrac{3}{4}E_0\), so \(\tfrac{1}{4}E_0 = \tfrac{3}{2}\) and \(E[\text{TT}] = 6\). Part B is a walk: the straight-line formula \[ P_i = \tfrac{i}{N} \] gives \(P_3 = \tfrac{3}{8}\). Naming the right tool — pattern wait vs. random walk — is the core skill.
hints: Part A counts flips until one pattern first appears. Part B is a chip drifting between two walls — different tools, different formulas.
Part A: does \(\text{TT}\) keep any progress after a lone T followed by a head? Compare \(\text{HH}\) vs \(\text{HT}\). Part B: use the straight-line law \(P_i = \tfrac{i}{N}\).
\(\text{TT}\) resets on a head just like \(\text{HH}\), so \(E[\text{TT}] = 6\). The walk: \(P_3 = \tfrac{3}{8}\).

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap cards to see how wait, race, and walk all connect.
beats.interviewNote: Mixed retrieval like this is the spaced-practice core of durable problem-solving: when the problem arrives unlabeled, you've rehearsed choosing the right tool (wait vs race, probability vs duration).

feedback.correct: Wait, race, walk — you can now tell them apart on sight.
hints: Pick the right tool per problem.
